coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Functionalized Carbon Quantum Dots as Fluorescent Probes for Detection of Cu2+
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2020 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: DENG, Xiang-Yi
FENG, Ya-Li
HE, Dong-Sheng
ZHANG, Zhen-Yue
LIU, De-Feng
CHI, Ru-An
coredata.dc:description: Polyethyleneimine functionalized carbon quantum dots (PEI-CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass tar as carbon precursor, which was produced in the reduction smelting process. Based on the selective fluorescence quenching effect between Cu2+ and PEI-CQDs, the accurate and rapid detection of Cu2+ was achieved. The results showed that as-prepared PEI-CQDs were spherical particles with uniform sizes, and the average particle size was 1.98 nm. Excellent dispersibility could be obtained in aqueous solution due to the hydrophilic functional groups (such as −COOH, −NH, −CN and C−O) on the surface of PEI-CQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs increased from 11.3% to 27.3% after PEI functionalization. Cu2+ could selectively interact with amino groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs by complexation, leading to the fluorescence quenching effect of PEI-CQDs. And the fluorescence quenching degree exhibited a linear relationship with the Cu2+ concentration ranging from 0.08 μmol/L to 400 μmol/L with a detection limit of 80 nmol/L. As fluorescent probes, PEI-CQDs have good accuracy and repeatability in Cu2+ detection, showing outstanding practical feasibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Functionalization
Fluorescent probes
Cu2+
Biomass tar

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 48, Issue 10, October 2020 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 ________________________ Received 8 April 2019; accepted 22 July 2020 *Corresponding author. Email: rac@wit.edu.cn This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1801800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21808176) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Wuhan Institute of Technology, China (No. 19QD58). Copyright Â© 2020, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(20)60054-8 RESEARCH PAPER Synthesis of Functionalized Carbon Quantum Dots as Fluorescent Probes for Detection of Cu2+ DENG Xiang-Yi1,3, FENG Ya-Li2, HE Dong-Sheng1,3, ZHANG Zhen-Yue1,3, LIU De-Feng1,3, CHI Ru-An1,3,* 1 School of Resources and Safety Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China 2 School of Civil and Resource Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China 3 Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China Abstract: Polyethyleneimine functionalized carbon quantum dots (PEI-CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass tar as carbon precursor, which was produced in the reduction smelting process. Based on the selective fluorescence quenching effect between Cu 2+ and PEI-CQDs, the accurate and rapid detection of Cu 2+ was achieved. The results showed that as-prepared PEI-CQDs were spherical particles with uniform sizes, and the average particle size was 1.98 nm. Excellent dispersibility could be obtained in aqueous solution due to the hydrophilic functional groups (such as â€’COOH, â€’NH, â€’CN and Câ€’O) on the surface of PEI-CQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs increased from 11.3% to 27.3% after PEI functionalization. Cu 2+ could selectively interact with amino groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs by complexation, leading to the fluorescence quenching effect of PEI-CQDs. And the fluorescence quenching degree exhibited a linear relationship with the Cu 2+ concentration ranging from 0.08 Î¼mol/L to 400 Î¼mol/L with a detection limit of 80 nmol/L. As fluorescent probes, PEI-CQDs have good accuracy and repeatability in Cu 2+ detection, showing outstanding practical feasibility. Key Words: Carbon quantum dots; Functionalization; Fluorescent probes; Cu 2+ ; Biomass tar 1 Introduction Copper is an important industrial raw material widely used in metallurgy, geology, chemical industry, material science and other fields due to the excellent mechanical properties and deformability. It is also one of the essential trace elements in human body participating in the physiological and pathological events [1,2] . The deficiency of copper in human body will affect enzyme activity and inhibit cell metabolism, leading to arterial diseases, high cholesterol and coronary heart diseases [3] . While, the excessive intake of copper will burden the liver, resulting in liver ascites, cirrhosis and other diseases [4,5] . Hence, it is of great significance to establish a highly sensitive and selective method for detection of Cu 2+ . The current detection assays of Cu 2+ , such as atomic absorption spectrometry [6] , inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry [7] , capillary electrophoresis [8] , and electrochemical method [9] , require tedious sample preparation procedures and expensive, sophisticated instrumentation. Thus, it is essential to develop simple methods for detection of Cu 2+ . As a low-cost detection method, fluorescence spectrophotometry is widely used due to its easy operation, high selectivity and sensitivity [10,11] . CQDs are fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with size less than 10 nm [12] . As a new promising carbon material, CQDs have attracted more attention because of its excellent light stability, chemical stability, water solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility [13] . CQDs show broad application prospects in the fields of medicine, biology and analytical chemistry [14,15] . In terms of metal ion detection, CQDs can be DENG Xiang-Yi et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 used as effective probes with high selectivity and sensitivity [16] . For instance, Zhang et al. [17] synthesized functionalized CQDs with N and S elements by microwave method using vitamin C as carbon precursor for detection of Fe 3+ . Liu et al. [18] prepared polyacrylamide functionalized CQDs by hydrothermal method using sodium citrate as carbon precursor for detection of Pb 2+ . The properties of synthesized CQDs by different raw materials and methods are quite different and the fluorescence quenching response to the detected substances is also different. Therefore, it is of great practical value to synthesis specific fluorescent CQDs using cheap and easily available carbon precursor by relative simple method. Biomass tar is an organic pollutant and a pyrolysis by-product in smelting processes using biomass, and contains harmful organic substances, such as carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and aromatics. Biomass tar can be used as a carbon precursor to synthesis CQDs. The synthesized CQDs may have fluorescent quenching response to many metal ions without functionalization. To obtain specific fluorescent recognition of Cu 2+ , in this work, PEI-CQDs were synthesized using biomass tar as carbon precursor and PEI as functional reagent by one-step hydrothermal method. Compared with other synthesis methods, such as laser ablation, microwave irradiation and pyrolytic degradation and so on [19,20] , this method had many advantages such as low cost, mild reaction conditions and simple operation. Furthermore, the fluorescence of as-synthesized PEI-CQDs could be selectively quenched by Cu 2+ . Based on this, a rapid and accurate fluorescence method for detection of Cu 2+ was established using PEI-CQDs as fluorescent probes. 2 Experimental 2.1 Instruments and reagents JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA), UV-1750 UV/vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), F2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) were used in this work. Distilled water was used throughout the experiment. The biomass tar used in the experiments was obtained by condensation and rotary distillation of the pyrolysis products of wood chips, which were generated in a nitrogen atmosphere at 500 Â°C. PEI was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All of the soluble metal salts (NaCl, MgCl2, KCl, CaCl2, MnCl2, Fe(NO3)3, CoCl2, NiCl2, CuCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2, and HgCl2) used in this work were of analytical reagent grade. 2.2 Synthesis of PEI-CQDs and CQDs PEI-CQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass pyrolysis tar as carbon precursor. The schematic for preparation of PEI-CQDs is shown in Fig.1. Specifically, 10 mL of 10 % biomass tar solution and 0.5 g of PEI were transferred into a 50-mL stainless steel autoclave equipped with Teflon. After dispersing by ultrasound for 5 min, the mixed solution was placed in a muffle furnace at 180 ÂºC Â± 2 ÂºC for 2 h. The brown solution containing PEI-CQDs was collected by filtering the resulting solution with a 0.22-Î¼m filter membrane, and then placed in 3500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove the redundant PEI reagent. The obtained PEI-CQDs were diluted to 100 mL by distilled water for further use and characterization. The synthesis of unfunctionalized CQDs was performed under the same conditions without the addition of PEI reagent. 2.3 Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield The fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate solution (sulfuric acid concentration of 0.05 mol/L) is 0.54 under 360 nm excitation light. The quantum yield of CQDs/PEI- CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as the standard (R) by the following equation [21] : Î¨ =ÏˆR(IAR/IRA)(Î·/Î·R) 2 (1) where, Ïˆ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated area under the emission spectrum, A is the absorption at the excitation wavelength, and Î· is the refractive index of the solvent. The refractive index is approximate in the same medium solution. And the quantum yield of CQDs/PEI-CQDs can be calculated by measuring I/A values at different concentration gradients of CQDs/PEI-CQDs and quinine sulfate. Fig.1 Schematic of preparation of PEI-CQDs DENG Xiang-Yi et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 2.4 Detection of Cu2+ PEI-CQDs solution (1.0 mL) was transferred into a 100-mL beaker, followed by adding a certain amount of Cu 2+ . Then the solution was diluted by PBS buffer (pH = 4.0) to 100 mL. Based on the fluorescence quenching effect between PEI-CQDs and Cu 2+ , the detection of Cu 2+ was achieved. The fluorescence quenching effects of other metal ions on PEI-CQDs were investigated by the same method as Cu 2+ . Each experiment was performed in triplicate. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Characterization of PEI-CQDs 3.1.1 The morphology of PEI-CQDs Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of PEI-CQDs is shown in Fig.2A. The as-synthesized PEI-CQDs were spherical with high dispersibility. The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) characterization revealed that PEI-CQDs had no obvious graphite-carbon lattice. The size histogram and Gaussian fitting curve (Inset in Fig.2A) showed that PEI-CQDs had a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 1 to 4 nm with an average particle size of 1.98 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescent intensity of PEI-CQDs in aqueous solution remained unchanged for more than 24 h, showing perfect stability and dispersibility, which was favorable for Cu 2+ detection. 3.1.2 Surface properties of PEI-CQDs Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded to identify the functional groups of the biomass tar and PEI-CQDs (Fig.2B). As shown in curve a in Fig.2B, the peaks at 3421, 3147 and 1643 cm â€’1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of â€“OH, Câ€“H, and C=O bonds [22] . The peaks at about 1400 cm âˆ’1 are because of the stretching vibration of C=C in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, revealing that the biomass tar contains harmful organic substances, such as carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and aromatics. In the spectrum of PEI-CQDs (Curve b, Fig.2B), the peak at 3246 cm â€’1 is assigned to Nâ€’H stretching vibration of the amide, the peaks at 2947, 2833 and 1302 cm â€’1 are ascribed to C-H stretching vibration, and the peak at 1645 cm â€’1 is attributed to C=O stretching vibration. The peaks in the range of 1575â€’1458 cm âˆ’1 are assigned to C=C stretching vibration of aromatic compounds. The peaks at 1111 and 617 cm â€’1 are ascribed to Câ€’N stretching vibrations and â€’NH2 twisted vibrations, respectively. According to FT-IR analysis, â€’OH stretching vibration vanished after hydrothermal treatment due to carbonization reaction. The appearance of N-containing functional groups, such as Câ€’N, Nâ€’H and â€’NH2, indicated that PEI was successfully functionalized on the surface of CQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was performed to further investigate the surface properties of the as-synthesized PEI-CQDs. The survey spectrum of PEI-CQDs shown in Fig.2C reveals that the peaks at 284.28, 399.39 and 528.57 eV are corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [23] . This Fig.2 (A) TEM image of PEI-CQDs, The insets show a HRTEM image and size distribution of PEI-CQDs; (B) FT-IR spectra of biomass tar (a) and PEI-CQDs(b); (C) XPS patterns of PEI-CQDs; (D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in PEI-CQDs DENG Xiang-Yi et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 result indicated that the PEI-CQDs were mainly composed of three elements(C, N and O), which was consistent with the results of FT-IR analysis. Figure 2D shows the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s in PEI-CQDs. Peaks at 284.35, 285.13, 285.31 and 287.24 eV were assigned to characteristic peaks of C=C, Câ€’O, Câ€’N, and C=O, respectively [24] . The appearance of N element indicated that PEI was successfully functionalized on the surface of CQDs. The results of FT-IR and XPS spectra revealed that hydrophilic groups such as â€’COOH, â€’NH, â€’CN and Câ€’O were functionalized on the surface of PEI-CQDs, resulting in the excellent dispersivity in water and providing operational feasibility for detection of Cu 2+ in aqueous solution. 3.2 Optical properties of PEI-CQDs 3.2.1 Effect of functionalization on quantum yield A linear relationship between integrated area (A) and absorption (I) could be obtained, as shown in Fig.3A. The I/A values of quinine sulfate, CQDs, and PEI-CQDs were 95.0 Ã— 10 5 , 19.9 Ã— 10 5 and 48.02 Ã— 10 5 , respectively. According to Eq.(1), the quantum yield of PEI-CQDs (27.3%) was much higher than CQDs (11.3%), demonstrating that the fluorescence property of CQDs could be significantly enhanced by PEI functionalization. Without PEI functionalization, the functional groups with sp 2 hybrid structures on the surface of CQDs, such as â€’OH, â€’COOH, could receive electrons excited by UV-light, resulting in the reduction of electron transitions between energy levels and decrease of fluorescence emission of CQDs [25] . The amino functional groups in PEI could react with â€“OH and â€“COOH. When PEI was functionalized on the surface of CQDs, the absorption photoelectron groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs decreased, while the surface defects increased, leading to the enhancement of fluorescence emission performance [26] . 3.2.2 Photoluminescence properties of PEI-CQDs The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of PEI-CQDs were recorded to investigate the photoluminescence properties of PEI-CQDs. As shown in Fig.3B, the characteristic UV-vis absorption peak appears at 340 nm, which indicates that PEI-CQDs can be excited at 340 nm to emit fluorescence. According to Fig.3B curve b, the maximal fluorescence excitation wavelength is 337 nm. This result coincided with the characteristic peak of UV-Vis absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig.3B, the characteristic wavelength of fluorescence emission is 460 nm. And the as-synthesized PEI-CQDs emitted bright blue light under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiation. Figure 3C shows the fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 380 nm. With the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 nm to 340 nm, the normalized fluorescence emission Fig.3 (A) Plot of integrated area vs absorption; (B) UV-Vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PEI-CQDs; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of PEI-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths; (D) Effect of pH value on fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs, Î»ex = 340 nm DENG Xiang-Yi et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 intensity increased rapidly from 0.27 to 1.0, and then decreased to 0.25 as the further increase of excitation wavelength. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm could be ascribed to Ï€-Ï€ * transitions of C=C unsaturated bonds [27] . Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission peak of PEI-CQDs shifted slightly from 460 nm to 479 nm, indicating that the emission spectra were dependent on excitation wavelength. 3.2.3 Effect of pH on fluorescence properties Figure 3D shows the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity (excited by 340 nm wavelength) of PEI-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity reached maximum at pH = 5.0, and then decreased slowly with the increasing of pH from 5.0 to 8.3. The â€’NH groups were introduced to the surface of CODs by functionalization of PEI reagent. And they could be deprotonated gradually with the increase of pH, promoting the transferring of photoelectrons and preventing recombination of excited state electrons and holes [28] . Thus the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs decreased as the pH increased. To obtain a strong fluorescence signal and ensure the ionic state of all metal salts, the pH was maintained at 3.0â€“5.0 by using PBS buffer solution (pH = 4.0) for detection. 3.3 Detection of Cu2+ using PEI-CQDs as fluorescence probes 3.3.1 Fluorescence response of PEI-CQDs to different metal ions To confirm the selective fluorescence response of PEI-CQDs to Cu 2+ , effects of 13 common metal ions (Na + , Mg 2+ , K + , Ca 2+ , Mn 2+ , Fe 3+ , Co 2+ , Ni 2+ , Cu 2+ , Zn 2+ , Cd 2+ , Ba 2+ , Hg 2+ ) on fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs were investigated. The fluorescence intensities of PEI-CQDs were measured at the optimum excitation wavelength (340 nm) with the concentration of 300 Î¼mol/L each metal ions. As shown in Fig.4A, only Cu 2+ significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CQDs, indicating the high selectivity of the PEI-CQDs for Cu 2+ in aqueous solution. The ratio of F to F0 (F/F0) can reflect the fluorescence quenching effect, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the PEI-CQDs at 340 nm without and with metal ions, respectively. The fluorescence quenching effect increased with the decrease of F/F0 value. The effect of the addition of Cu 2+ on F/F0 value in the presence of different metal ions is shown in Fig.4B. The F/F0 values (the red) decreased significantly after adding Cu 2+ to the solution, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs could be strongly quenched by Cu 2+ in the presence of different ions. Furthermore, the little fluctuation of F/F0 values indicated that the as-synthesized PEI-CQDs had anti-interference ability for detection of Cu 2+ . 3.3.2 Detection Cu2+ Figure 5A shows the fluorescence quenching curves of different concentrations of Cu 2+ . The relative fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs decreased from 1.0 to 0.32 with the Cu 2+ concentration increasing from 0 to 400 Î¼mol/L. The normalized fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increasing concentration of Cu 2+ , showing the feasibility of Cu 2+ detection using PEI-CQDs as fluorescence probes. According to Stern-Volmer theoretical equation, F0/F = 1 + KsvCq (where, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and Cq is the concentration of the quencher (Cu 2+ )), the standard curve (excitation wavelength of 340 nm) of Cu 2+ detection was obtained as shown in Fig.5B. The F0/F showed an excellent linear relationship with the Cu 2+ concentration in the range of 0.08â€“400 Î¼mol/L with calibration equation as F0/F = 0.8686 + 0.0054[Cu 2+ ] (Î¼mol/L) (R 2 = 0.998). The detection limit of Cu 2+ (3Î´/S, where Î´ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot) was estimated to be 80 nmol/L. The detection performance of the present method was compared with other reported CQD-based fluorescent probes for Cu 2+ detection, as shown in Table 1. The proposed method had wide linear range with an acceptable detection limit, showing good application perspective for Cu 2+ detection. Fig.4 (A) Effect of different metal ions (300 Î¼mol/L) on fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs (excited at 340 nm); (B) F/F0 values before (black) and after (red) addition of Cu 2+ in the presence of different metal ions DENG Xiang-Yi et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 Fig.5 (A) Fluorescence quenching curves of different concentrations of Cu 2+ ; (B) Calibration curve for detection of Cu 2+ Table 1 Comparison of performance of CQD-based fluorescent probes for detection of Cu 2+ CQD-based probes Detection limit Linear range Ref. CQDs from leeks 0.05 Î¼mol/L 0.01â€“10 Î¼mol/L [29] CQDs from petroleum coke 29.5 nmol/L 0.25â€“10 Î¼mol/L [30] Ionic liquid derived CQDs 180 nmol/L 0.5â€“5 and 10â€“90 Î¼mol/L [31] Cyclam-functionalized CQDs 100 nmol/L 0.7â€“4.0 Î¼mol/L [32] TPEA-CQDs 100 nmol/L 1â€“60 Î¼mol/L [2] CQDs from pakchoi juice 9.98 nmol/L 0â€“100 nmol/L [33] PEI-CQDs 80 nmol/L 0.08â€“400 Î¼mol/L This work 3.4 Fluorescence quenching mechanism of PEI-CQDs-Cu2+ system Figure 6 shows the mechanism of PEI-CQDs fluorescence probes for detection of Cu 2+ . The electrons on the surface of PEI-CQDs are excited by UV light to high-level orbits. When the excited electrons return back to ground state, the electron/hole radiation recombination is formed and fluorescence emission can be observed. However, Cu 2+ can interact with the amino groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs by coordination. When the electrons are excited by UV light, they can be transferred from PEI-CQDs to Cu 2+ , thus effectively preventing the radiative electron/hole recombination and resulting in fluorescence quenching effect in the PEI-CQDs-Cu 2+ system. To further verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, Fig.6 Fluorescence quenching mechanism of PEI-CQDs in the presence of Cu 2+ fluorescence quenching and recovery experiment were carried out by adding Cu 2+ and EDTA-2Na successively. As shown in Fig.7A, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CQDs is significantly quenched by Cu 2+ (Fig.7A, curve b), and then recovered by 20 Î¼mol/L of EDTA-2Na (Fig.7A, curve c). As a metal chelating agent, the chelating ability of EDTA-2Na is stronger than that of PEI-CQDs. When EDTA-2Na and PEI-CQDs coexist in the same system, the Cu 2+ combined with PEI-CQDs will be chelated with EDTA-2Na, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was further verified to be the complexation between Cu 2+ and PEI-CQDs. The complexation constant between PEI-CQDs and Cu 2+ can be calculated by Benesi-Hildebrand [34] as follows: 1/(F0 â€’ F) = Ka[(F0 â€’ Fmin)[Cu 2+ ]] + 1/(F0 â€’ Fmin) (2) where, Ka is the complexation constant and Fmin is the lowest fluorescence intensity quenched by Cu 2+ . The complexation constant Ka can be calculated by the plot of 1/(F0 â€’ F) vs 1/[Cu 2+ ] (Fig.7B). The slope of fitting linear equation was 345.44 and the intercept was 0.412. According to the Eq.(2), the complexation constant Ka was calculated to be 838.45. 3.5 Real sample detection The applicability and accuracy of PEI-CQDs for detection of Cu 2+ in real sample generated by acid leaching of oceanic manganese nodules in our lab were investigated. The atomic absorption spectrometry method was also used to verify the results. As shown in Table 2, with this method, the recoveries of Cu 2+ ranged from 96.6% to 102.1% and all of the relative DENG Xiang-Yi et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2020, 48(10): e20126â€“e20133 Fig.7 (A) Fluorescence spectra of different systems; (B) Plot of 1/(F0 â€’ F) vs 1/[Cu 2+ ] Table 2 Detection of Cu 2+ using this method and atomic absorption spectrometry Method Added (Î¼mol/L) Found (Î¼mol/L) Recovery (%) RSD (%, n = 3) This method 0 100 95.35 95.4 2.5 150 145.85 97.2 1.7 200 204.72 102.4 0.8 Atomic absorption spectrometry 0 100 95.39 95.4 1.3 150 148.76 99.2 2.1 200 204.69 102.3 1.1 standard deviation (RSD) values are less than 2.5%. And the recoveries of Cu 2+ ranged from 95.4% to 102.3% with the RSD values of less than 2.1% using atomic absorption spectrometry method. The results of these two methods had no significant difference, confirming the accuracy and repeatability for detection of Cu 2+ in real samples using this method. 4 Conclusions PEI functionalized CQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass tar as carbon precursor. The synthesized PEI-CQDs were spherical particles of uniform size with an average particle size of 1.98 nm, which could emit bright blue fluorescence under UV light. The fluorescence yield was obviously improved due to the increase of surface defects by PEI functionalization. The hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs made them dispersible in aqueous solution. Furthermore, Cu 2+ could selectively interact with PEI-CQDs probes by complexation, leading to the fluorescence quenching. The F0/F showed an excellent linear relationship with the Cu 2+ in the concentration range of 0.08â€“400 Î¼mol/L with a detection limit of 80 nmol/L. A rapid and accurate fluorescence spectrophotometric method for Cu 2+ detection was established, proving that PEI-CQDs could be used as effective fluorescence probes for detection of Cu 2+ in real sample. References [1] Zhang S R, Wang Q, Tian G H, Ge H G. Mater. Lett., 2014, 115: 233â€“236 [2] Qu Q, Zhu A W, Shao X L, Shi G Y, Tian Y. Chem. Talanta, 2007, 71(3): 1130â€“1135 [9] Rahman M A, Won M, Shim Y. Anal. Chem., 2003, 75(5): 1123â€“1129 [10] Deng X Y, Li J Y, Tan K J. Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2014, 42(4): 542â€“546 [11] Liu X P, Yang J, Bai Y. Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2016, 44(5): 804â€“808 [12] Xu X Y, Ray R, Gu Y L, Ploehn H J, Gearheart L, Raker K, Scrivens W A. J. Am. Chem. ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1801800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21808176) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Wuhan Institute of Technology, China (No. 19QD58).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Functionalized Carbon Quantum Dots as Fluorescent Probes for Detection of Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Safety Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Safety Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil and Resource Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil and Resource Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil and Resource Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of Ministry of Education, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyethyleneimine functionalized carbon quantum dots (PEI-CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass tar as carbon precursor, which was produced in the reduction smelting process. Based on the selective fluorescence quenching effect between Cu2+ and PEI-CQDs, the accurate and rapid detection of Cu2+ was achieved. The results showed that as-prepared PEI-CQDs were spherical particles with uniform sizes, and the average particle size was 1.98 nm. Excellent dispersibility could be obtained in aqueous solution due to the hydrophilic functional groups (such as −COOH, −NH, −CN and C−O) on the surface of PEI-CQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs increased from 11.3% to 27.3% after PEI functionalization. Cu2+ could selectively interact with amino groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs by complexation, leading to the fluorescence quenching effect of PEI-CQDs. And the fluorescence quenching degree exhibited a linear relationship with the Cu2+ concentration ranging from 0.08 μmol/L to 400 μmol/L with a detection limit of 80 nmol/L. As fluorescent probes, PEI-CQDs have good accuracy and repeatability in Cu2+ detection, showing outstanding practical feasibility.

Amino groups on the surface of PEI-CQDs can interact with Cu2+ ions by complexation selectively, and the electrons in excited states transfer from PEI-CQDs to Cu2+ ions, leading to the fluorescence quenching effect.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang S R, Wang Q, Tian G H, Ge H G. Mater. Lett., 2014, 115: 233–236

Wang X R, Li B W, You H Y, Chen L X. Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2015, 43(10): 1499–1504
